eli3: Expectation (mean) is the long-run average value: E[X] = sum of (value × probability). For a fair die: E[X] = (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 3.5. It's the 'center of mass' of the distribution.

Variance measures spread: Var(X) = E[(X - E[X])²] = average squared distance from mean. Standard deviation is √Var — same units as original variable. Small variance = concentrated; large variance = spread out.

Linearity of expectation is powerful: E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y], always (even if X and Y dependent). But Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) only if X, Y uncorrelated. Covariance measures how X and Y vary together.

whenWhy.title: Loss functions and risk minimization
whenWhy.body: MSE = E[(Y - Ŷ)²] is expected squared error. MAE = E[|Y - Ŷ|]. Expected loss drives decision theory. Bayes risk is minimum expected loss over decisions. Value at Risk (VaR) is quantile of expected loss.

whenWhy.title: Portfolio theory and risk
whenWhy.body: Expected return = weighted sum of asset returns. Portfolio variance depends on covariances: σₚ² = wᵀΣw. Diversification reduces variance when assets aren't perfectly correlated. Mean-variance optimization balances return vs risk.

whenWhy.title: Bias-Variance tradeoff
whenWhy.body: MSE = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Error. Understanding expectation and variance decomposition is crucial for model selection, regularization, and ensemble methods.

whenWhy.title: A/B testing and experimental design
whenWhy.body: Compare E[Y|Treatment] vs E[Y|Control]. Standard error of mean estimate depends on Var(Y)/n. Power calculations require specifying expected effect size and variance.

deepDiveIntro: Expectation is the probability-weighted average: for discrete variables, sum each value times its probability; for continuous variables, integrate value times density. Expectation exists whenever the absolute values have finite expected value. Linearity of expectation — that the expected value of a sum equals the sum of expected values — holds even for dependent variables, with no independence required.

Variance is the average squared deviation from the mean. An equivalent formula — expected value of the squared variable minus the square of the mean — is often easier to compute. Covariance measures how two variables move together; dividing by both standard deviations gives correlation, bounded between -1 and 1. Independence implies zero covariance, but the converse is false — zero correlation does not imply independence.

deepDive.heading: Expectation (Mean)
deepDive.body: Expectation is the probability-weighted average over all possible values. It is linear — you can pull constants out and split sums — regardless of whether the variables are independent. To find the expected value of a function of a random variable, you can plug the function directly into the weighted average without first deriving the distribution of the function (the law of the unconscious statistician).
deepDive.formula: E[X] = Σ x p(x)  or  ∫ x f(x)dx

deepDive.heading: Variance and Standard Deviation
deepDive.body: Variance measures how spread out a distribution is — it is the average squared distance from the mean. Scaling a variable by a constant squares the variance. For two variables, the variance of their sum includes a covariance term; only when they are uncorrelated does variance simply add. The standard error of the sample mean — how precisely the sample mean estimates the population mean — is the standard deviation divided by the square root of sample size.

deepDive.heading: Covariance and Correlation
deepDive.body: Covariance measures whether two variables tend to be above their means at the same time (positive covariance) or in opposite directions (negative covariance). Dividing by both standard deviations normalizes this into correlation, which is always between -1 and 1. Correlation equals ±1 only when the two variables are in a perfect linear relationship.
deepDive.formula: Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y],  ρ(X,Y) = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y)

deepDive.heading: Moments and Moment Generating Functions
deepDive.body: Moments summarize the shape of a distribution: the first moment is the mean, the second central moment is the variance, the third (standardized) captures skewness, and the fourth captures tail heaviness (kurtosis). The moment generating function encodes all moments in a single function and uniquely identifies the distribution when it exists. All moments can be recovered by differentiating the MGF at zero.

deepDive.heading: Conditional Expectation
deepDive.body: The conditional expectation of Y given X is the expected value of Y restricted to cases where X takes a particular value — it is the regression function. The law of total expectation says the overall mean equals the average of conditional means. The law of total variance decomposes total variance into the average within-group variance (unexplained) plus the variance of group means (explained) — the same split used in ANOVA.

takeaways: E[X] = Σ x p(x) or ∫ x f(x)dx. Long-run average, center of mass.
Var(X) = E[(X-μ)²] = E[X²] - μ². Spread, quadratically scaled.
Linearity of expectation always holds. Var(X+Y) needs independence (or correction for covariance).
Conditional expectation E[Y|X] is the regression function.

edgeCases.a: If E[|X|] = ∞, expectation is undefined. Example: Cauchy distribution (t with 1 df) has no mean — it's symmetric with heavy tails. Sample means of Cauchy don't converge (violate LLN). Stable distributions with α < 1 have no mean.
Yes. Example: X ~ Uniform(-1,1), Y = X². Cov(X,Y) = E[X³] - E[X]E[X²] = 0 - 0 = 0 (uncorrelated). But Y is completely determined by X (dependent). Correlation only captures linear relationships.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between variance and standard deviation?
edgeCases.a: Variance is in squared units (harder to interpret). SD is in original units (e.g., if X is in dollars, SD is in dollars, variance in dollars²). SD is used for confidence intervals and describing spread. Variance is mathematically nicer (adds for independent variables, quadratic form).

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
